coredata.dc:title: Homogeneous and highly photoluminescent composites based on in-situ formed fluorophores in PVA blends
coredata.dc:creator: Mai, Xuan-Dung
Mai, Van-Tuan
Nguyen, Van-Quang
Nguyen, Xuan-Bach
Hoang, Quang-Bac
Doan, Dieu-Thuy

Duong, Hong-Quan
Pham, Hung-Vuong
Nguyen, Van-Hao
Duong, Ngoc-Huyen
coredata.dc:description: There is a strong demand for developing a simple, scalable, and transferable method to prepare homogeneous and highly photoluminescent composite for diverse applications. Herein, homogeneous composites were successfully synthesized by thermal annealing blends of polyvinyl alcohol, citric acid, and ethylenediamine. The composites exhibited intensive photoluminescence under excitation around 350 nm. Infrared, nuclear resonance, and optical characterizations suggest that diverse structures involving 5-oxo-1,2,3,5-tetrahydroimidazo-[1,2-α]-pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (IPCA) accounts for the high photoluminescence of the composites.

One-pot synthesis
Fluorophore
IPCA
PVA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The evolution of absorption (a) and PL (b) spectrum of PLC with annealing time. Inset in (b): pictures of PVA and PLC under UV lamp. c) PL and d) excitation spectra of 4 h-annealed PLC.

a) The separation of PLC into desorbed product (Des) and remade membranes (Rec). PL spectra of Des (b), reference sample (Ref) (c), and Rec (d). Absorption e) and excitation f) spectra of interested samples.
a) FT-IR and b) H-NMR spectra of desorbed product (Des) and reference (Ref) sample.
The formation (1) IPCA, (2) IPCA bounded to PVA polymer, and (3) IPCA decorated polymers.

head.ce:title: Homogeneous and highly photoluminescent composites based on in-situ formed fluorophores in PVA blends

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One-step thermal treatment homogeneous blends of PVA, citric acid, and ethylenediamine results in composites that emit intensively under UV bumping. The origin of absorption, excitation and emission properties of composites was investigated by combining optical and chemical characterizations. It has been demonstrated that IPCA, being involved in diverse structures accounts for the optical properties of composites.

There is a strong demand for developing a simple, scalable, and transferable method to prepare homogeneous and highly photoluminescent composite for diverse applications. Herein, homogeneous composites were successfully synthesized by thermal annealing blends of polyvinyl alcohol, citric acid, and ethylenediamine. The composites exhibited intensive photoluminescence under excitation around 350 nm. Infrared, nuclear resonance, and optical characterizations suggest that diverse structures involving 5-oxo-1,2,3,5-tetrahydroimidazo-[1,2-α]-pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (IPCA) accounts for the high photoluminescence of the composites.

ce:section.ce:para: Blending a photoluminescent (PL) component and a polymer emerges new valuable properties such as reduced photobleaching [1], fine-tuning refractive index [2], and optical response which are suitable for diverse applications [3-6]. One of the greatest challenging issues in preparing photoluminescent composites (PLC) is the aggregation of the PL component originated from differences in the Hansen solubility parameters [7-9]. Bonding quantum dots to polymers [1,7,10,11], physical mixing water-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [12,13] or in-situ formation of PL components in a polymer matrix [14,15] have been advised to obtain homogeneous PLC. Recently, the formation of fluorophores, e.g. 5-oxo-1,2,3,5-tetrahydroimidazo-[1,2-α]-pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (IPCA) and their vital role to the photoluminescence of hydrothermally synthesized CQDs have been demonstrated [16-19]. Notably, IPCA is an intermediate product formed at mild temperatures and retained partially in final CQDs. The mechanism suggests a cost-effective pathway to synthesize PLC if the fluorophore prototype could be formed within a polymer matrix.

Herein, homogeneous PLC were successfully prepared by one-pot annealing blends of PVA, CA (citric acid) and EDA (ethylenediamine) at 150 °C and it was demonstrated for the first time that diver structures involving IPCA account for high photoluminescence of PLC.

An aqueous solution containing PVA, CA, and EDA was dried on a hot plate to form homogeneous blends which were further annealed at 150 °C to obtain PLC. Please see Supporting Information for detailed synthesis of PLC, reference samples, and characterizations.

Based on thermogravimetric analysis we annealed blends of PVA, CA, and EDA at 150 °C, which was sufficient to induce condensation between CA and EDA while PVA remains unchanged (Fig. S1). The absorption spectra shown in Fig. 1 a exhibited an absorption band maximized at 355 nm whose absorbance increased with annealing time and an absorption tail that was observed above 400 nm in long-time annealed samples. The absorption band was only observed when both CA and EDA were added to the blends (Fig. S2). PL spectra shown in Fig. 1b are composed of two emission centers at 447 nm and 503 nm. As the annealing time prolonged, the former’s intensity decreased while the latter’s intensity increased. In the 4 h-annealed PLC, (Fig. 1c and Fig. S3) the green emission center was dominant when excited below 340 nm or above 400 nm. PLE spectra observed at different wavelengths, as indicated in Fig. 1d, were similar and resembled to the absorption profile indicating that the aforementioned emission centers originated from the same absorption source. Notably, the optical properties of PLC and composites made of CQDs and PVA were alike [1].

While studying the water resistance of PLC, we unexpectedly observed a highly photoluminescent product desorbed from PLC. To figure out its chemical origin, we separated PLC into desorbed product (Des) and remade membranes (Rec) by dialysis (molecular cut off: 20000 Da) (Fig. 2 a). A reference sample (Ref) was prepared from CA and EDA using a hydrothermal method at 150 °C for comparison with the Des; the results are summarized in Fig. 2 and Fig. 3 . PL spectra of Des and Ref shown respectively in Fig. 2b and 2c were almost the same, exhibiting excitation-independence and PL maximum at about 470 nm. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 2e and 2f, Des and Ref display alike absorption and excitation spectra. It has been widely documented that under mild hydrothermal conditions, e.g. 150 °C, CA and EDA undergo intermolecular dehydration processes results mostly IPCA (Fig. S4) [16,17,20]. FTIR spectra of Des and Ref shown in Fig. 3a exhibit absorption peaks at 1712 cm−1 and 1670 cm−1 which could be attributed to CO in carboxyl (HOCO) and amide (NHCO) groups, respectively [20]. X-ray photoelectron spectra of Des and Ref (Fig. S5) confirm the existences of C in the forms of CC (284.4 eV), C–O/C–N (286.7 eV), O–CO (288.6 eV) [21]; N in pyrrolic (399.5 eV) and graphitic (401.5 eV) forms [22]; and O in CO (351.1 eV) and C-O (532.9 eV) structures [23]. Furthermore, Des and Ref show similar proton nuclear magnetic resonance (H-NMR) spectra which exhibited four types of proton in IPCA as noted in Fig. 3b. Two peaks at ca. 2.2 and 2.4 ppm could be assigned to H in methylene groups of intermediate polymers that were formed by intramolecular dehydration between CA and EDA (Fig. S4) [24,25]. As shown in Fig. 2d–f, Rec displayed high photoluminescence and having similar absorption and excitation profiles to the original PLC. Additionally, the absorption and PLE spectra of PLC or Rec were similar to those of either Res or Ref with an exception that the absorption/excitation band was red-shifted by about 12 nm in composites.

Based on the above discussion we proposed a tentative formation mechanism of PLC as cartooned in Fig. 4 . Upon thermal annealing, CA reacts with EDA forming amides which may further undergo intermolecular reactions. This process resulted in IPCA while condensation between the amides and remaining CA/EDA molecules produced polymeric structures [16,23,25]. Within PVA medium, available –OH groups facilitate esterification with –COOH group of IPCA forming IPCA decorated PVA [1,15]. The formation of IPCA accounted for the resolved absorption band at 355 nm (Fig. 1a and S6) whose absorbance was saturated after 12 h of annealing due to the limited diffusion of CA/EDA precursors in PVA matrix. The absorbance above 400 nm observed in 24 h-annealed PLC could be due to π-π stacking of IPCA (Fig. S5) or bonding of IPCA to other conjugated systems [16,26–28]. When exposed to water, light-weight structures involved IPCA were desorbed as Des, while heavy polymers containing IPCA retained in composites as Rec. The similarities between Des and Ref, discussed above in Fig. 2 and Fig. 3 indicate that the condensation dehydration of CA and EDA into IPCA and other structures was akin to that in the traditional hydrothermal synthesis. The absorption redshift of IPCA in PLC (shown in Fig. 2e) could be due to close interactions between IPCA units or inhomogeneous H-bonding between IPCA and PVA medium [29]. The distinct absorption peak and excitation band of PLC were retained in Rec indicating that IPCA involved in diverse structures dominated the optical properties of PLC.
Highly photoluminescent composites were successfully prepared by thermal annealing blends of PVA, CA, and EDA. The absorption and emission properties of composites originate from IPCA.
IPCA included in diverse structures in-situ formed in PVA matrix account for the optical properties of composites.

Xuan-Dung Mai: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Software. Van-Tuan Mai: Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Van-Quang Nguyen: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xuan-Bach Nguyen: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Quang-Bac Hoang: Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft. Dieu-Thuy Doan: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Anh-Duc Vu: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Xuan-Viet Do: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Hong-Quan Duong: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Hung-Vuong Pham: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Van-Hao Nguyen: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ngoc-Huyen Duong: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by Vietnam Ministry of Education and Training, Hanoi Pedagogical University 2 under project no. B.2022-SP2-04.
